Good evening!

I hope that your October is off to a good start.

1. This might very well be a fine theme for meditation, but I'm trying to better understand the link (or lack thereof) between the astral plane and physical space. My understanding is that astral phenomena can affect things over great distances, but also that at least some of them can be linked to physical locations. Is there a relatively brief explanation of this and/or a source that discusses it worth checking out? Or am I stuck working this one out for myself :)?

2. I just read Bishop Joseph Hall's The Art of Divine Meditation since you recommended it at some point as a place to go after the basic instructions for discursive meditation in most magic books. Some of his techniques seem rather Christian-specific (such as the phase of meditation where you complain about your lack of/inability to attain the desired spiritual quality). Any thoughts on what of his approach you have found broadly useful versus what must be altered for different spiritual traditions?

3. What are your thoughts on the modern American celebration of Halloween? Consumerist nonsense? Authentic cultural expression? Harmless fun? As close as some folks can get to having a spiritual relationship with death? Something else?

4. Asking for a friend, of course: there's been a lot of discussion here and on the main blog about why it's a bad idea to sexually fantasize about real-life people. I'm wondering at what point thoughts/imaginings become harmful - is there potential negative astral effect from an appreciative glance? A passing lewd thought? I doubt there's a bright line or anything, but if there's any principle besides "the more thought and energy you put into it, the stronger an effect", I'm curious what it might be.

5. Possibly a question for the commentariat: Presumably, Sun Salutations are good for welcoming and distributing solar energy through the (subtle) body. Are there any Hatha Yoga asanas or sequences of asanas that would be good to balance that out with some telluric energy?

As always, thanks very much,
Jeff

replies.content:
As an American, my own take on Halloween is that it is entirely consumerist
nonsense. If it was ever a cultural expression, that fell by the wayside long
before my time (I'm 67). Like many other holidays, it came over here with the
Europeans as part of All Saints Day, etc. As with Christmas and Easter, it has
degenerated into a candy/gift filled economic bonanza with little of the original holiday
left.

One interesting tid-bit is that the practice of kids dressing up in costumes and
trick-or-treating from door to door is relatively recent. My late mother (born 1920)
says no one did that here in the New England area when she was a kid. Apparently it
got popularized when Jack Benny did an episode about it on his radio program (before TV).
There's little originality in the costumes now. It's all movie related stuff, Spiderman,
Thor, Batman. Even the scarier stuff is Hollywood derived (Joker, Pennywise et al).

Covid has put a big dent in all that, at least around here. I don't bother buying
candy any longer because so few trick-or-treaters show up now and I don't want to
get stuck with eating it myself. I can see much of the consumer-age Halloween withering
away over the next decade or so. Whether Halloween itself will ever regain its
original spiritual/cultural aspects is anyone's guess.

JLfromNH/Crimson Deranged Kraken

Another factor in rise of adult participation in Halloween is that the Gay community embraced it as a time that they could cross dress safely. Well into the 70s many jurisdictions had laws against people appearing in public in the garb of the opposite sex and those laws were enforced arbitrarily. Halloween, and to a lesser extent, New Years parties that were expected to be masquerade events were opportunities to do drag safely. Then straight people decided it was fun to be "sexy witch" "sexy pirate" "sexy nurse" etc.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1. This is an absolutely enormous question. I've been working on it on and off for several years now. As JMG stated, the main connections are formed through the etheric, and every connection between the two does end up having an etheric influence too. Despite that, it seems that certain phenomena go from astral to physical or physical to astral and only afterwards affect the etheric. Catching onto certain geometric relationships can induce powerful physical changes related to structuring certain physical motions along the basis of those relationships. Similarly, careful use of the imagination can induce the physical body to behave in some really useful but atypical ways; like increasing elasticity of the tendons or causing non-dilatant deformation of soft tissue. The reverse relationship (p->a) appears in the context of most ritual postures and yoga poses; the effect is really hard to observe since it requires you to watch two things at once.

The best way I've found to study it so far is to pick a mental or physical phenomena, hold it in your attention for a time, then try to trace it into the ether, then continue into the next plane. The borders between the planes seem to have a distinctive and discrete feeling. Breathing exercises and reciting verbal formulae have yielded the best results in these experiments so far.

5. Sun salutations are actually balanced and don't need to be partnered with any other asanas. The cobra pose and downward facing dog are strongly aligned to telluric forces. Crescent moon pose is explicitly understood to be medial in other contexts, it just isn't usually stated in this one.

comments.title: Philosophy, mythology, science
comments.content:
I seem to recall that you recommend an aspiring mage study philosophy, mythology, and natural science. Assuming that is correct (apologies if I misremembered something):

1. I've recently taken up studying Stoicism and Aristotelianism for philosophy. I just couldn't find any decent intros to Neoplatonism, and kind of gave up. But I want to make sure I've taken up decent philosophical schools as part of my larger magical/occult education.

2. I'm not sure what natural science to tackle first. Do you recommend one that is particularly suitable for an aspiring mage?

3. I'm not sure where to start for mythology. I have considered comparative mythology, perhaps between Christianity and the predecessor Pagan religions. Would that be helpful or do you have any other recommendations?

replies.title: Re: Philosophy, mythology, science
replies.content:
1) Instead of looking for an intro to Neoplatonism, look for an intro to the work of Plotinus. His Enneads are challenging, but there are quite a few good introductions to them.

2) Depends on what kind of magic you want to do. Natural magic? Botany is essential. Alchemy? A good background in laboratory chemistry can save your life. Any kind of healing work? You need to know anatomy and physiology. Druid magic? Ecology, first and foremost. The list goes on. What kind of magic do you have in mind?

3) Don't read about mythology, read mythology. Get a good book of myths from a tradition that appeals to you and dive in.

1) In hindsight, that seems so obvious that I kind of feel a little silly for not thinking of it.

As for next steps, if you like the standard LBRP, you might consider the course of training in my book Learning Ritual Magic.

Look up the length of the day from sunrise to sunset -- you can find this on time and date websites among other places -- and mutiply the number of hours by 60, then add in the minutes. Divide that by 12 to give the length of each hour. The hours go in descending order -- Saturn - Jupiter - Mars - Sun - Venus - Mercury - Moon -- and you begin at sunrise with the planet whose day it is, i.e. the Sun on Sunday, the Moon on Monday, and so on. You work out the planetary hours of the night the same way, but you start with the interval from sunset to sunrise.

2) I had occasion to write such a program recently, so I can explain how planetary hours work. Call the time interval between local sunrise and sunset the day, and the time interval between local sunset and the following local sunrise as the night. Divide each day or night into twelve equal slices of clock time, and assign to these slices their lords in rotation, beginning with Saturn as the lord of the first hour of the day of Saturday. You will find that, around the equinoxes or near the equator, planetary hours are about the same length as clock hours. Nearer the solstices and away from the equator, planetary hours in the longer of the day or night will be somewhat longer than clock hours, and planetary hours in the other will be compressed likewise. Feel free to experiment.

In the October 4 data from Om, you can see the day hours compressed slightly due to the days just after the equinox being a little shorter than the nights, exaggerated due to the distance from the equator. Those data are plausible and, to within a rounding error, agree with my usual go-to site for a planetary hour list, planetaryhours.net. I try to allow a minute or two on either side of a planetary hour to account for time, location, and computational uncertainties.

Hope this helps,
Dreary Nocturnal Llama

comments.title: Just Art, or Occult Folly?
comments.content:
Just art, or occult folly?

The Rollright Trust describes the installation as follows:

"The Totem 'Latamat' (meaning 'The Life) was carved by the indigenous Totonac artist Jun Tiburcio and is a message to the world from the Totonac people to draw attention to the damage being done to the world and environment.
The message conveys how our existence is tightly interwoven with nature, and how we cannot go on ignoring the damage and destruction that is being inflicted on the world."

Not knowing anything about much I looked up Totonac gods for clues on symbolism...
One wonders if the Rollright Trust have read anything about the Gods and Goddesses of the Americas:

"Chief among the gods of the Totonacs was the sun god. They dedicated the main temple at Cempoala to this god. The sun god was married the corn goddess. While the sun god demanded human sacrifice, the corn goddess demanded animal sacrifice in her rituals accompanied by flowers and herbs. Other important Totonac gods include the Old God of Fire, represented as an old man wearing a rather weighty headpiece, and another god referred to as Old Thunder. Old Thunder was god of all the waters except for the rain.
He wanted to flood the world because drowning victims became his minions in the afterlife.
The Totonac rain god is very similar to Tlaloc, the old god of the Aztecs associated with rain. Many other gods were tied to places or the elements and are represented in the many figurines unearthed at Totonac sites."
https://mexicounexplained.com/the-totonacs-a-forgotten-civilization/

And:

"At the winter solstice an important festival was celebrated during which eighteen people, men and women, were sacrificed. Eighteen is also the number of veintenas (Span., "set of twenty," i.e., "months" composed of twenty days each) into which the Mesoamerican year was divided. The human sacrifice took place at midnight; the hearts of the victims were ground into the mouths of the principal idols. Blood was the food of the Sun."
https://www.encyclopedia.com/environment/encyclopedias-almanacs-transcripts-and-maps/totonac-religion


Since much of the damage being done to the world is driven by dysfunctional activities of humans, it strikes me that this could have unexpected consequences and blowback!

I don't know - I'll have to consider this more, but when I first read about it my initial reaction was "Are you sure this is a good idea?"

The fluffy description:
"The message conveys how our existence is tightly interwoven with nature, and how we cannot go on ignoring the damage and destruction that is being inflicted on the world."

...brought initial questions to mind:
1. What is the actual message to which they refer?
2. What kind of relationship might humans have with such deities?
3. Didn't the Roman Catholic Church do an installation with a Meso-American deity/symbol?

There is more basic detail here:
https://originsfestival.bordercrossings.org.uk/programme/totem-latamat

When I read about the Totonacs, and started to think about the subject, a strong impression came to mind (and I really, really hope this is just imagination working overtime on a pulp fantasy fiction story) but the imagery that came to mind re:

>> The human sacrifice took place at midnight; the hearts of the victims were ground into the mouths of the principal idols. Blood was the food of the Sun."

...was to wonder about myocarditis and spike proteins protruding from endothelium.

Too many crappy science fiction and fantasy stories as a kid would be a nice comfortable head in the sand conclusion - but the seemingly insane rush to inject as many people as possible alongwith Meso-American deities with a penchant for blood sacrifice turning up in this interesting manner, well, that's just added a level of weirdness to an already extraordinarily weird world!

One other thing - when I started to think on this matter, I had a strong feeling not to focus on the totem/Totonacs but to concentrate on how I am using the central practice from the Dolmen Arch and to build on that instead.
An old teacher always used to say to us - "energy follows focus" - and my gut says it's time to be very careful what I focus on.

Any thoughts appreciated.

replies.title: Re: Just Art, or Occult Folly?
replies.content:
This whole thing struck me as a very, very bad idea. I have no objection whatsoever to Native American art works being displayed elsewhere, so long as it's with the permission of the maker and the relevant tribal elders, but bringing sacred items from one tradition into a setting that's sacred to a very different tradition -- as Pope Francis did, of course, and as the Rollright Trust is doing -- can have very messy results.

As for the broader issue, you'd need to find out about Totonac (and native Mesoamerican) religion now, rather than what it was five centuries ago. Five centuries ago, after all, many Christian churches were as enthusiastic about human sacrifice as the Totonacs -- yes, they called it punishing witches, heretics, and Jews, but it amounted to the same thing in practice.

replies.title: Re: Just Art, or Occult Folly?
replies.content:
So; to quote Hunter S Thompson: "When the going gets weird, the weird turn pro."

JMG: "As for the broader issue, you'd need to find out about Totonac (and native Mesoamerican) religion now, rather than what it was five centuries ago"

From the little I've seen, some mesoamerican religions have incorporated/hybridized things with some of the ideas from Catholicism, but I am still left with questions about the nature of the deities - just because people want something does not mean deities might take any notice or care a jot.

I don't know; I guess the next 6 months might bring some clarity (or not!)
:)

As for the 'bad idea' - that was what struck me too... but I don't know why; we shall, perhaps, see.

replies.title: Re: Energy Blockage
replies.content:
If I may (and please note that the following (non-magical) is advice I am fully credentialled, licenced and authorised to give): ;)

Such an energetic blockage pattern is known in Traditional Chinese Medicine as "plumstone qi" - ie trapped "qi" (or energy) in the throat that "feels like" a plumstone stuck there that cannot be swallowed. If you wish to try some acupressure treatment on yourself, the following instructions may give you some relief, at least.

I am sure you can find online location guides to the following acupressure points:
1) PC6,
2) PC5 (these two are located on the inner aspect of your forearm)
3) LV3
4) LV2 (these two are located on the dorsum of your foot)

These are points known to have some effects on helping move the specific qi blockage known as "plumstone qi".

If you wish to try this, use any online location guide to get into "roughly" the right area, then press all around that area until you feel an actual soreness. Often the feeling you get will be a bit like finding out that you have a tiny, sharp-edged bit of grit or stone trapped a little deeper than, the skin. You may find that some, all or maybe even only one of these points produces this feeling. The feeling is important, because that is what confirms you are on an "active" (therefore useful) point.

Your job then is simply to press - nothing fancy. Press down for a minute, relax for a minute, press again and so on for 3-7 presses. (Use only the points that have shown themselves to be sore to press, and vary them from one session to another). Then wait a while and see if it has helped. If so, repeat as needed. If the condition resolves, you should find that one or more of the "active" points become less tender and sensitive to your pressure.

Anyway, FWIW, I hope your lockdown ends soon. Best, Scotlyn

This was posted earlier in the covid thread, and asked to be held over until Monday.

https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/150722.html?thread=19460546#cmt19460546

replies.title: Re: Incarnation
replies.content:
Okay, this is going to take a deep plunge into magical theory (which is why I asked you to bring it here, rather than responding to it in the Covid open post).

Demons are souls left over from the universe before ours, beings so unbalanced that they were not able to complete their spiritual evolution in their own cycle of time and were left behind. They can't incarnate because of the resulting mismatch -- it's a little like trying to breed a bulldog and a petunia, in a certain sense. Possession is as close as they can get, and that typically kills the possessed person after a certain amount of time unless the demon is exorcised.

As for why they would want to incarnate, remember that they're very weak. They can only contact us because of certain serious mistakes made early on in the history of our species, which are dimly recalled in the legend of the Fall and similar stories from some other traditions, and all they can do is wheedle, tempt, bully, and trick. They can't even obsess or possess someone unless that person consents to it. If they could get physical and etheric bodies, they would be much more able to do things.

As it is, they are being brought slowly back into balance by their experiences in this universe, and the best thing we can do is banish them and leave them to the process of their own slow evolution.

Would the jab itself be considered consent though an individual might not know what they consent to? And if "informed" consent is not required, then how innocuous a form could a consent take? Would persistent lack of impulse control or a focus on self-gratification be enough of an entryway?

Again, thank you for your time and efforts here.

replies.title: Re: Incarnation
replies.content: No, you're taking what I said in a broader sense than it was meant. Obsession and possession are not the only ways demons and humans interact, after all. If someone summons a demon and asks the demon to do something in the human world -- for example, interfere with an election or overturn a presidency -- they've given their consent for the demon to take action in the human world. The demon would be limited by a range of factors, not least that those who are influenced by him would have to accept the influence, though they might well not know where it came from. But when you have thousands of people practicing evil magic and invoking demons because they're filled with rage and hatred toward their political enemies -- and that's a fair description of the Magic Resistance, after all -- you've got a lot of demonic energy spilling out into a very broad section of the population, many of whom have not merely consented to demonic influence but (by invoking demons) actively sought it out.

comments.title: Ogham discrepancy

replies.title: Re: Ogham discrepancy
replies.content: This should probably go into the FAQ section. Surviving accounts from Ogham, which date from Christian times, give the order of letters in the first aicme (set of five) as BLFSN. Scholars of an earlier generation, who I think were right (though their ideas are unfashionable now), suggest that the original order was BLNFS; since the F is pronounced V, that spells BeLeNVS, Belenus, the name of a Celtic Pagan god -- which is why, according to these scholars, the order was changed by Christian monastic scholars. So you can make up your own mind which one to use!

comments.title: Daimones
comments.content:
So I've been cagey about bringing this into the conversation, especially with the Demonic Influence theory and mentions of... Cacomagie? Is that right? (Sorry, not familiar with that. I don't conjure demons, I just play pen-and-paper RPGs).

But since it was mentioned awhile back that the Greeks often see things as they are, not as ideals; and because these stories have been haunting me for awhile without reprieve, I guess I might as well stick my neck out.

Why not Ατη?

Theoi link: https://www.theoi.com/Daimon/Ate.html

Ατη is the personification of rash action, recklessness and ruin. The surface level myths that I found of her have her doing Hera's bidding, goading a lover of Dionysus to attempt to ride a bull (which kills him) and getting Zeus to oath kingship based on birth order, which Hera then manipulates behind the scenes to give to her favored candidate (ie son of the woman who -didn't- lay with her husband). When Zeus casts Ατη down from Olympus to plague mankind, he sends his feeble-footed daughters the Litae to chase after her; however Ατη never need touch the earth as she speeds along the heads of man, leaving the Litae to clean up the messes her presence has spawned.

I found it specifically interesting, that the few myths I found where Ατη herself is acting, she uses social pressure in particular. She convinces, she cajoles, she uses peer pressure. Her walking upon the heads of men speaks to me particularly as an explanation for mob mentality...

Other translations present in the site are Error (her roman counterpart too), Delusion, and Blind Folly.

The parentage of Ατη is attributed to either Zeus (in the fallen olympian persona) or to Eris (in lists of Kakodaimones) but never both. I wonder at that particular pairing of influences, if not deities. An unpleasant being as many Kakodaimones are, her name sometimes translated to Ruin itself, but also born of the zealous and expansive (Jovial?) personality that is Zeus. I mean, does the guy ever know when to stop? He might have cast folly down, but with his innumerable extramarital liaisons only one example, I doubt he really rid himself of her.

As an alternative to the perpetrator of ruin, she also referenced as a bringer of consequence alongside the Furies.

The final bit I'll say on this is that while we can observe around us tragedy and pain, old age, back-breaking work for minimal reward, even murders... these are not things we separate from the human condition. They can be abhorrent, but they are not "other". So why, why, why do we see the strange concert of fear and hatred and delusion around us as something with a higher cause, or else somehow less than
...human?
---------------------------

But that's what I have. Bring on the character assassinations, how dare I bring a particular Daimon/Daemon/Demon here, I must be a daemonolator, deluded by Delusion... I'm certain I've already turned over any criticisms of my capability in my head a thousand times.

Otherwise, what does everyone else have to add, what arguments can be made for and against? More info is better to the discerning mind, even if my mind is not among them.

Regards,
Addie Ilion

comments.title: Unexpected Effect from Working Through "Learning Ritual Magic"
comments.content:
JMG:

I am working through LRM (halfway through), and I am finding myself more sympathetic to Christianity.

A bit of history, I grew up vaguely Catholic because my parents while sincere in their Catholocism are not really fervent in it. I also grew up on the South and in Texas. In Texas especially, during my time in high school, I had the opportunity to be exposed to the far more fervent and far less sincere evangelical movement. The hypocrisy of the Church hierarchy (the parish priest was always a hit or miss affair, and mine had far more hits than misses) and evangelicals being themselves really turned me off Christianity. In my late teens I also got a hold of Regardie's "Golden Dawn" book and began practicing magic but was not ready. Although, the Golden Dawn material held my interest in Christianity for a little while longer.

As I am working though LRM however, I find that I have become, or maybe 'returned' is the right word, more sympathetic to Christianity. Two or three times I have questioned whether I should return. I doubt I will for a number of reasons. Notably, I am a polytheist, I do not feel Christianity is the path for me, and I still don't like the way congregations conduct themselves.

But this leaves the question for me: why the weird change in heart after all these years? On the one hand, I think it is a broadening of my own perspective; that is, seeing the value that can be had in Christianity and the influence it has had on me whether I liked it or not. Christian symbolism was what I was raised with after all and is probably inextricable from my world view at least in this life. On the other hand, I worry whether practicing in a magical tradition rooted in Christianity draws me back whether I like it or not.

I am curious whether other people like me have had the same experience of rethinking Christianity, including having moments of thinking of rejoining, when practicing Golden Dawn style magic.

replies.title: Re: Unexpected Effect from Working Through "Learning Ritual Magic"
replies.content: Yes yes yes! I've experienced this too. I've never really forsaken Christianity and the Christian god but with my experiences in the Christian and non christian community I'd stay away from both. I've also  felt more sympathetic to other pantheons as well. Kinda like joining the two in a way.

replies.title: Re: Unexpected Effect from Working Through "Learning Ritual Magic"
replies.content:

-copper

replies.title: Re: Unexpected Effect from Working Through "Learning Ritual Magic"
replies.content: Huh I have two more lessons in LRM left and had basically the same experience; I grew up Catholic as well.  I even went through a couple weeks of praying the rosary daily and had good results.  Ultimately I knew the results were because of the foundation built by magic which I'm definitely not willing to give up for Catholicism.  Dogma does not suit me.  But yes, that sentiment of participating in the Church nagged me on and off for awhile.

replies.title: Re: Unexpected Effect from Working Through "Learning Ritual Magic"
replies.content:
There's a lot of that happening these days, for reasons rooted in collective psychology. Every civilization has its own Age of Reason, and every Age of Reason eventually collapses into moral and intellectual bankruptcy; ours is doing that right now. When that happens, a lot of people who bought into whatever form of rationalism was fashionable find their way back to the traditional faith of their culture; that's beginning right now, beginning with dissident intellectuals. As it happens, because our minds aren't locked up inside our skulls, the movement of people back toward familiar religious ideas sets up an emotional undertow of the kind that generally drives mass religious events, and a lot of people get swept up in it whether they expect to do so or not. So that's what you're feeling.

As for what to do about it -- well, if you don't plan on becoming a Christian, invoke your gods and ask for their help and protection. The Second Religiosity, which is what Spengler calls the process now unfolding in Western societies, is dry and autumnal compared to the mighty religious passions of earlier eras; you can stand outside it without too much trouble if you choose to.

replies.content:
My daughter is at least 2nd generation nonbeliever. Her paternal grandfather took his children to "church," a polite, respectable, expected social ritual as far as I can tell, and so, to her dad, all religion was filed under "church." Her husband's mother has rejected all faiths because their congregations were no better than any other people. (The Other Grandmother's mind doesn't work like most people's, so I give her a pass on what sounds like arrogance but didn't feel like it.)

She told me last Thanksgiving that she could not believe in any gods, but felt the need to give thanks, so her husband found some Native American stuff, real or fake, online, giving thanks to nature in its different aspects.
Since mine is also a nature spirituality, it felt right to me, but the source bothered me. Because, if real, they should have had permission from the tribe. If fake,...never mind. You've said in other contexts that claiming sources that were false is commonplace and harmless, if rather silly. Am I correct?

The question is, since it felt like the first stirrings of what they know to be my own faith, is this how the Second religiosity would be working out in her and her family?

replies.content: Interesting. Not everyone moves back toward the tradtional religion of the culture, and since North America is beginning to evolve its own very distinctive religious sensibility, one that's focused on nature, they may be beginning to tune into that. As for fake sources, it's no problem at all -- people have been claiming fake sources for their own spiritual innovations since roughly a week before the dawn of time.

comments.title: Different Bodies
comments.content:
Hello!
Could you help me understand the different bodies the human has? Can you explain the Astral Body, Etheric, Spiritual (is that a thing?) and any others. What are these and how they're used. Also do Auras have anything to do with these.
Thank you,
E.L.

replies.title: Re: Different Bodies
replies.content:
That's material for a good-sized book! The very short form, however, is that there are different planes of being, and each of them has its own substance, the way that matter is the substance of the material plane. Human beings have bodies made of some of them. On the material plane, obviously, we have material bodies, the body of meat, bone, blood, and so on that we're all familiar with. On the etheric plane we have etheric bodies -- these are bodies of vital energy or life force, and the meridians that acupuncturists use are in that body. On the astral plane we have astral bodies, which are egg-shaped and extent well out from the surface of the material body -- that's the aura.

Those are the three planes where we have bodies. We're in the process of evolving a fourth body, a mental body, on the mental plane. So far all we have are mental sheaths, which are rudimentary forms that don't yet have special centers and organs. We don't yet have bodies at all on the spiritual, causal, and divine planes, and long ages of further evolution will have to pass before we can achieve those.

replies.title: Re: Different Bodies
replies.content:
Not op

Would we still be physically human, or hominid, by the time we evolve mental, causal and divine bodies?

replies.title: Re: Different Bodies
replies.content: We won't even be incarnate. Our swarm of souls, as I understand it, has the capacity to have three bodies at a time. When we finish evolving a mental body we'll shed the physical body for good and operate on the mental, astral, and etheric planes. As we evolve higher bodies we'll keep shedding lower ones, until at our final stage of evolution, when we rank alongside the Lords of Flame, Form, and Mind -- "angels" is not a bad description -- we'll have mental, spiritual, and causal bodies only, around the Divine Spark that is the essence of the self.

replies.title: Re: Different Bodies

Even if the restriction does not apply, it occurred to me just now that what higher messages, manifestations, or whatever I have apparently received might be divided into different kinds basically corresponding to lower and upper astral (maybe occasionally something like etheric as well), at least as I understand them, but I *can* look for mental level ones as well. Would that be a good idea?

replies.title: Re: Different Bodies
replies.content: Beings who have completed their evolutionary journey can act on any plane -- they just do so in a disembodied form. It's polite to rise to the highest plane you can reach in order to address them, and the mental level tends to be much clearer a channel for communication than the planes below it, so yes, it would be a good idea.

comments.title: Demons in Fiction
comments.content:
Hello, JMG; I've got a question. You've made it quite clear that actually dealing with actual demons is a very bad idea, and I believe you there; while I don't recall it coming up, I suspect it's also a bad idea to "mock" deal with supposedly fictional demons, since there's probably something out there that would notice what one was doing and be quite happy to start the lies off with one about the name. My question is whether there's any special caution one should take regarding demons, fully fictional or based on historically known demons, just as significant characters in stories. I assume, remembering your anecdote about that thing with Batman, that contemplating them excessively could still lead to bad places, but are there any special cautions that should be used beyond that?

(This question was brought to mind mostly by the web show "Helluva Boss" and related materials; since it's colored dots moving about on a glass sheet, I don't expect you're familiar with it directly, but I thought that you or some other commenter here might have heard of it.)

replies.title: Re: Demons in Fiction
replies.content:
I've been thinking about the same thing, recently. The fantasy genre in every medium has a lot of fictional "demons." Thankfully, they rarely correspond to the lore of actual demons, and are more like wrathful gods and other malevolent spirits. That's not necessarily something you want to involve yourself with too often or too powerfully, but it's probably not going to mess up your life — and afterlife — in the same way. Even the occasional fictional depiction of an entity clearly written as a demon — as you might find in the occult detective genre — isn't likely to do significant harm, as long as work also depicts more positive spiritual forces.

I'm not familiar with "Helluva Boss," but I assume it uses vaguely Christian devil imagery. Satan, like Ba'al, presents an interesting case, because both the name of both a demon and a deity/angel — and in both cases, the deity/angel came first and was demonized. That doesn't make the demon any less real — spirits have a habit of responding to whatever name we call them. I wish I could help you more, but I'm still musing on that sort of case myself.

replies.title: Re: Demons in Fiction
replies.content: I'd recommend avoiding any use of actual demonic names and characteristics. It's safe to invent them out of whole cloth, and easy:  I present you with Morthvael, Ninth Prince of the Abyss, a lean haggard figure the color of bleached bones with long sinister claws and a vile, hissing voice. (I just made him up.) Fully fictional critters like this are no more dangerous in the real world than Snidely Whiplash.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
So...I wanted to ask about something weird that has been going on in my life and might have a possible magical dimension.

Back in 2014, I had a dream that I had decided to become a Mormon. Being a directionless grad student at the time, and unhappy with my then-current church, I took this as a sign that I should at least consider Mormonism and, the next day, idly walked into a building on campus that had "Latter Day Saints" written on it (turned out it was an "Institute of Religion"-basically a community center for Mormon college students) and asked the first person I saw just what their church was about.

The Mormons reacted to this with a speed and zeal I frankly could not have imagined. I was quickly hooked up with missionaries, given a Book of Mormon to read, and then the next week, when I said that it indeed seemed to me like a wholesome text that might have come from God, was "challenged" to agree, on the spot, to set a date for my baptism. Being a socially awkward Autistic person, I didn't really know how to say "no" to people-especially those nice, earnest Sister Missionaries who so desperately wanted me not to. So I wound up getting baptized into the Mormon Church.

Of course, not long after this, in the course of researching my new religion online, I found a number of problematic aspects of the church's history, and began to notice that the church itself was a very close-knit, but also very strict and controlling organization. The sense I that I had jumped (or been pressured to jump) before even looking down to see if the pool had water in it began to grow more and more acute, reinforced by my family and the my former Orthodox priest. Eventually, about a month after being baptized and two months after meeting the missionaries, I tore myself away from the church and wrote a letter asking to be officially removed as a member, which they did. The whole thing felt, at the time, like an extremely traumatizing experience-talking with another priest about it, he said something like "I think you dodged a bullet", and inwardly I was thinking "no, I got shot".

Ever since then, the Mormon church has had a hold on me that I have been unable to rationally explain. I kept feeling the urge to go back, and did a couple times, though always the memory of my earlier experience would come flooding through and I would stop. I drifted between different churches, but felt unable to be at home in any of them. Eventually, over the past year or so, I let my practice of daily prayer more or less stop.

Then, about a month ago, I had someone I knew become involved in...well, a major crisis situation, and I prayed that they would get out of it, which they did. About a week later, a Mormon missionary contacted me on Facebook. I asked her how she'd gotten my name and she said she had not gotten it from anywhere, and was from a ward (Mormon parish) I had previously had no contact with. She told me that she'd simply prayed about who she most needed to talk to, then looked at a random list of names on Facebook and clicked mine. Thinking that this might be some kind of synchronicity, and realizing I'd been unable to have a real relationship with God in the years since 2014, I started talking to her, and wound up agreeing to go back to church.

And then the story basically repeated itself. The Mormon church turned out to be...well, the same one I'd joined and left in 2014, and I found myself being "challenged" to agree on a "target date" to be rebaptized (I managed to say "no" this time). Eventually, last week, I decided that it was time to tear myself away from the Mormon church, again-and immediately upon making this decision, I was overwhelmed with a massive flood of sadness, followed by a thought that seemed almost insane-that somehow, this sadness was not my emotion, but was coming into me from something else. Afterwards, even after having resolved not to, I felt this constant...well, almost compulsion to apologize to the missionaries and get my behind back the LDS church where it belonged-until, this morning, another almost insane thought hit me-that the emotions I was having were almost exactly those of the narrator at the very end of Lovecraft's story A Shadow Over Innsmouth, when he finds out he's related to the fish people and feels a compulsion-seemingly from outside his normal personality-to join them.

What exactly is happening here? In agreeing to get baptized into the Mormon church and going through with it, have I irrevocably tied myself to that organization? Is it possible that the Mormon church has an egregore, or perhaps a spirit guiding it, that's just as pushy as it's earthly representatives and has somehow latched onto me? And what could I do about it? Its occurred to me that, if this really is what I think it is, perhaps taking up a regular LBRP practice again might help? Or maybe I'm just psyching myself out.

replies.title: Re: Human self loathing
replies.content:
The crucial thing is to remember that the opposite of one bad idea is another bad idea. The fixation of humanity's supposed awfulness is the natural result of the equal and opposite fixation on humanity's supposed wonderfulness, and both are wrong.

Look for the point of balance in the middle. When you find such thoughts rising, mentally label them as unbalanced and incomplete. Remember that humanity can't be categorized in any simplistic way -- for every bad thing about us there's a good thing about us, and vice versa. Practice that and over time you can find the middle ground.

comments.title: tightaxe
comments.content:
I asked my yoga teacher about stiffness in my hips and legs, which is apparently related to my backside. He thought about this and came up with a response in the next class (spelling changed per forum rules)-

If you're having flexibility issues bending forward try to give away something every day. Your axe could be tight because you are being a tightaxe.

Well after driving around with a box of donations in my car for 2 months I finally made it to the drop box. Still I had difficulty dumping it all in. It's like a muscle I never use, and when it came time to get rid of the stuff I got all weird about it. This probably has something to do with being the kind of person who repurposes everything I can, but I was not previously aware of the psychological/physical effects of carrying such a large inventory. It's a significant mental burden trying to keep track of it all, and a real drain to constantly trip over things I don't need but am too stubborn to give away.

If it works I will report back. Maybe this will helps someone else.

replies.title: Re: tightaxe
replies.content: That makes a great deal of sense to me. Have you ever met someone who was a pain in the neck, and ended up after the encounter with a genuine pain in the neck?

comments.title: Ascended Masters Teachings
comments.content:
Some Magic Mondays ago, JMG mentioned something called the Ascended Masters Teachings, which he said were friendly to political conservatives. Over the weekend, in a moment of curiousity, I decided to see if I could chase these teachings down-and every website I managed to find was associated with a group called the Church Universal and Triumphant (or CUT). Now I've heard a little bit about the CUT-not much granted, but from what I do know they kind of seem like a cult. Is this impression correct? And JMG, by "Ascended Masters Teachings", did you mean the CUT's body of doctrine, or something else?

Also, has anybody had any experience with a denomination called Divine Science? They have a church in my city, and so far they're the only real New Age/Western Mystery Tradition-oriented group I've been able to find and physically go to. Their denomination seems to be built around New Thought (indeed, they even recognized what New Thought was when I asked them about it), and they gave me a copy of the Kybalion out of their church library to read. On the other hand, they seem to have about the same membership base as your typical Episcopal church-ie, 75% PMC women-and I recognized signs of the the degenerate, PMC type of New Thought that JMG has sometimes warned about (The Universe loves you! You're the most specially special snowflake! Accept Abundance into your life right now!-yes, they use that word). Having gone though a lot of hardship in the past few years, I have a somewhat surly disposition towards that kind of thing, and I'm divided on whether to make that church my spiritual home or not.

replies.title: Re: Ascended Masters Teachings
replies.content: The CUT is an offshoot of the Ascended Masters teachings -- loud and rather cultish. The Saint Germain Foundation is the original group, weirder but less problematic. There are also a lot of smaller groups. As for the Church of Divine Science, it's classic New Thought, and yes, they're up to their eyeballs in abundance thinking -- you'll have a hard time finding one that isn't.

replies.title: Re: Ascended Masters Teachings
replies.content:
Divine Science was founded by Malinda Cramer in San Francisco way back in the middle 1880s, in consequence of a mystical exprience she unexpectedly had. It's one of the few branches of New Thought that was arose independently of Emma Curtis Hopkins. Eventually Cramer encountered Mary Baker Eddy's Christian Science, and was influenced somewhat by it.

Cramer herself died in 1906 of injuries suffered during the Great San Francisco Earthquake and Fire, but her teaching was carried on and developed by the Brooks sisters (Nona and Fannie) in Denver, Colorado. It's still around, but -- like most New Thought churches -- has moved somewhat toward New Age ideas. (Unlike Christian Science, New Thought was generally receptive to borrowings from Eastern wisdom and spiritual traditions. One might even argue that New Thought was the parent of our current New Age movement.
